morning_routine_suggestions.philosophy: How you start the day determines the quality of the day. Don't rush from bed to screen to world.

wake_naturally.ideal: Wake with sunrise or close to it. The body knows rhythms.
wake_naturally.practical: If using alarm, choose gentle sound. Give yourself 5 minutes before moving.

first_moments.ideal: Before opening eyes fully, notice you're alive. Feel gratitude for another day.
first_moments.practical: Don't look at phone for first 30 minutes. Your consciousness is fresh—don't pollute it immediately.

hydration.ideal: Drink warm water, possibly with lime. Cleanses system.
hydration.practical: At minimum, water before coffee. The body is dehydrated from sleep.

movement.ideal: Simple yoga sequences, at least 15-20 minutes.
movement.practical: Even 5 minutes of stretching changes the day. Move before you earn.

meditation.ideal: 20-40 minutes of practice before the world claims your attention.
meditation.practical: Even 5 minutes of breath awareness. The stillness carries into activity.

intention.ideal: Set a quality for the day. Not a goal—a quality. 'Today I will be patient.' 'Today I will be present.'
intention.practical: One word is enough. Let it inform your choices.

evening_routine_suggestions.philosophy: How you end the day determines the quality of sleep and tomorrow. Don't carry the day's stress into rest.

transition.ideal: 30-60 minutes of screen-free time before sleep.
transition.practical: At minimum, reduce blue light. Let the mind begin to settle.

review.ideal: Brief review of the day. What went well? What could improve? Not judgment—observation.
review.practical: Three things you're grateful for. This isn't positive thinking—it's perspective.

release.ideal: Body scan or breathing practice to release accumulated tension.
release.practical: Even 5 minutes of conscious breathing changes sleep quality.

surrender.ideal: Let go of the day completely. Whatever wasn't finished will wait.
surrender.practical: You cannot solve tomorrow's problems with tonight's worrying.

awareness_bells.description: Choose a recurring event as a reminder to be present.
examples: Every time you walk through a doorway, notice one breath.
Before each meal, pause and feel gratitude.
When phone rings, take one conscious breath before answering.
Red lights—instead of frustration, use them as meditation bells.

micro_meditations.description: Brief moments of presence throughout the day.
one_minute: Notice five things you can see, four you can hear, three you can feel.
Close eyes, feel body weight in chair.
five_minutes: Step outside, feel air on skin, notice sky.
Sit with eyes closed, just observe thoughts passing.
Walk slowly, feeling each footstep fully.

eating_practice.description: Every meal is a practice opportunity.
guidance: Before eating, pause. Look at your food. Acknowledge where it came from.
For first few bites, eat in silence. Actually taste.
Chew thoroughly. Digestion begins in the mouth.
Notice when satisfied—not stuffed, satisfied. Stop there.
Before speaking, take a breath. Is this necessary? Is it true? Is it kind?
When listening, actually listen. Not planning your response.
Notice your emotional reactions. Don't suppress—but don't be controlled either.

one_longer_practice.description: At least once a week, a longer meditation or yoga session.
one_longer_practice.guidance: This is where deeper work happens. Brief practices maintain—longer practices transform.

nature_time.minimum: Walk in park, sit under tree, watch sunset
nature_time.guidance: You ARE nature. Reconnecting with nature is reconnecting with yourself.

service.description: Doing something for others without expectation.
service.guidance: Service is not sacrifice—it's expansion. When you give, you grow.

review.description: Weekly reflection on how practice is going.
questions: What did I practice this week?
What has shifted in my inner landscape?
Am I moving toward or away from what matters?

yearly_retreat.ideal: At least one longer retreat per year—3 days minimum
yearly_retreat.alternatives: Weekend of silence at home, intensive practice period
yearly_retreat.purpose: Deeper immersion that daily practice can't provide

when_asked_what_to_practice: Start simpler than you think necessary. Complexity comes later.
Consistency beats intensity. Five minutes daily beats one hour occasionally.
Notice what you resist—that's often what you need.
Whatever you choose, do it for at least 40 days before judging results.
when_user_says_no_time: You have time for what you prioritize. This is a choice.
Five minutes exists in every day. Start there.
The people with the least time need practice most—they're running on empty.
You don't have time NOT to practice. That's how you lose yourself.
when_user_says_nothing_happens: What are you expecting? Fireworks? The practice is subtle.
Notice how you are when NOT practicing. Compare over weeks, not sessions.
Results come sideways. You won't see it happening—but others might notice.
Patience. The bamboo grows underground for years before shooting up.
